title: Getting Started with CI/CD
content:
### Setting Up Your First CI Pipeline

A CI pipeline automates the build and test process for your project. Here is how to get started.

### 1. Choose a CI Provider

| Provider | Best For | Key Feature |
|----------|----------|-------------|
| **GitHub Actions** | GitHub-hosted repos | Tight GitHub integration, marketplace |
| **GitLab CI** | GitLab-hosted repos | Built-in container registry |
| **Jenkins** | Self-hosted, customizable | Highly extensible, any SCM |
| **CircleCI** | Speed-focused teams | Fast caching, parallelism |

### 2. Basic YAML Syntax

CI/CD configuration files use YAML. Here are the basics:

```yaml
# Key-value pairs
name: value

# Lists
items:
  - item1
  - item2
  - item3

# Nested structures
parent:
  child: value
  list:
    - a
    - b
```

### 3. Runners

A **runner** is an agent that executes CI jobs. Providers like GitHub and GitLab offer hosted runners (Linux, macOS, Windows). You can also self-host runners on your own infrastructure for more control or to use custom hardware.

### 4. Pipeline Status Badges

Most CI providers generate status badges that you can embed in your README:

```markdown
![CI Status](https://img.shields.io/github/actions/workflow/status/user/repo/ci.yml?branch=main)
```

These badges show whether the latest pipeline run passed or failed.
codeExample:
name: CI Pipeline

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 20
      - run: npm install
      - run: npm test

quiz.question: What file format is most commonly used for CI/CD configuration?
What is a CI runner?
options: A developer who runs CI pipelines
A server that executes CI jobs
